name: Kosava: Puslowski Palace & Kosciuszko Museum
description: I always bring guests here when I want to show them that Belarus has stories worth a film — a neo-Gothic palace, a freedom fighter's birthplace, and a small town that carries all of it quietly.

practical.how_to_get_there: By car from Brest (~100km). No direct public transport.

name: Olman Swamps
description: Most people I know have never heard of Olman — and that is exactly why I want you to go. This is Polesia at its most raw, where Belarus feels ancient and completely unhurried.

practical.how_to_get_there: By car from Pinsk (~60km). Guide recommended.

name: Sporava Marsh
description: If you want to hear Belarus rather than see it, come to Sporava in the morning — the reeds, the birds, the stillness. It is one of those places that stays with you long after you leave.

name: Yelnya Mire
description: Yelnya is over 9,000 years old and most Belarusians have never been — I want to change that. Walking across floating moss above dark peat water is unlike anything else this country offers.

practical.how_to_get_there: By car from Miory (~12km). Visitor centre in town.

name: Polotsk Old Town & Saint Sophia Cathedral
description: If you want to understand where Belarus begins as a nation, start in Polotsk — this is where our statehood, our faith, and our identity were first shaped, and it never feels like a museum.

practical.how_to_get_there: Train or bus from Vitebsk (~100km) or Minsk (~260km).

name: Zdravnevo Estate Museum
description: I love bringing people here because almost nobody expects to find Repin's estate in Belarus — it is quiet, personal, and it shows a side of our cultural history that deserves far more attention.

name: Druya & Bernardine Monastery Ensemble
description: Druya sits at the edge of the country and feels like it belongs to several worlds at once — Belarusian, Polish, Jewish, Catholic. Come here if you want to feel how layered this land really is.

name: Turov Meadows
description: Every spring this floodplain becomes one of the great birdwatching places in all of Eastern Europe — I find it hard to believe more people do not know about it, and I want you to be one of those who does.

name: Turov
description: Turov is one of those towns where you feel the weight of time — pilgrimage, river life, and old local traditions all woven together in a way that most Belarusian towns have long forgotten.

name: Yurovichi Archaeological Complex
description: People lived here 26,000 years ago — let that sink in. Yurovichi connects Belarus to a story far older than any castle or cathedral, and almost nobody talks about it.

name: Vetka Museum of Old Believer Culture
description: For me it is one of the most distinctive museums in Belarus — when I was a child I often heard stories about Old Believers but never understood who they are. Now I want to share with you how they shaped this region's identity.

practical.how_to_get_there: By bus or car from Gomel (~24km).
practical.what_to_bring: Nothing special — the museum provides everything.

name: Krasny Bereg Estate & Memorial
description: I have never left Krasny Bereg without feeling something — the elegance of the manor and the weight of the wartime memorial sit side by side in a way that is impossible to forget.

name: Blue Krynica Spring
description: There is something about this spring that draws people back again and again — pilgrimage, folklore, and the simplest kind of natural beauty all in one place that feels genuinely sacred.

practical.what_to_bring: A bottle to take water home.

name: Bychov Synagogue Area
description: Bychov does not try to be polished — and that is why I find it so honest. Military history, Jewish heritage, and a town that is still figuring out its own story. Worth every minute.

practical.how_to_get_there: By car or bus from Mogilev (~60km).

name: Chigirinskoye Reservoir
description: I discovered this place looking for somewhere to SUP away from the usual spots — big open water, big sky, and almost nobody around. That is still the best way I can describe it.

name: Murovanka Church
description: The first time I saw Murovanka I could not quite believe it was real — a fortified medieval church that looks like it was built to survive a siege. Belarus surprises you when you let it.

practical.what_to_bring: Camera — you will want to photograph this from every angle.

name: Krevo Castle Ruins
description: Krevo has never been restored and I think that is its greatest quality — these walls saw the Grand Duchy of Lithuania at its height, and they still carry that history without any polish over it.

practical.how_to_get_there: By car from Minsk (~100km) or Molodechno (~35km).
practical.what_to_bring: Good footwear — the ground is uneven.

name: Krasnoselsky Chalk Quarries
description: I showed these quarries to a friend who thought I was taking him to a beach in Greece — the water is that colour. It is industrial, unexpected, and completely Belarusian all at once.

practical.what_to_bring: Swimwear, sunscreen — the water is safe for swimming.

name: Lake Svityaz
description: Svityaz sits quietly in the far west with clear water, old legends, and almost no crowds — I think of it as the lake Belarus keeps to itself, and I am happy to share it with you.

name: Naliboki Forest
description: Naliboki is one of the largest forests in Belarus and I think it is one of the least appreciated — come here to hike, cycle, and remember that this country is not only about monuments.

practical.what_to_bring: Hiking boots, bike if you have one, map.

name: Stracha River
description: I paddled the Stracha for the first time and did not want it to end — quiet forest, hidden lakes, and a pace that makes you forget there is anywhere else to be. One of my favourite routes in the country.

practical.how_to_get_there: By car from Minsk (~70km). Kayak rentals available locally.
practical.what_to_bring: Kayak or canoe, waterproof bag, camping gear for multi-day.

name: Berezinsky Biosphere Reserve
description: You will hear Berezinsky before you see it — cranes at dawn, beavers on dark rivers, a forest that has never been rushed. This is Belarus stripped back to what it was before anything was built on top of it.

practical.how_to_get_there: By car from Minsk (~120km). Visitor centre at Domzheritsy.

name: Budslau Basilica
description: Budslau is one of the clearest expressions of living pilgrimage culture in Belarus — for me it represents the heritage, history, and multiculturalism of our beautiful country.

practical.best_season: Year-round. Major pilgrimage in early July.

name: Loshitsa Estate
description: When I want to show someone the Minsk that does not appear in guidebooks, I bring them to Loshitsa — a manor, a legend, and a park that feels worlds away from the capital's avenues.

practical.how_to_get_there: By metro to Mogilevskaya, then 15 min walk.

name: Svislach River Urban Kayak Route
description: I grew up in Minsk and paddling the Svislach made me see my own city completely differently — from the water, it stops being Soviet and starts being a river city with its own quiet rhythm.

practical.how_to_get_there: Central Minsk — kayak rental points along the river.

name: Tsnyanskaye Reservoir Watersports Area
description: This is where Minsk people come to breathe — SUP boards, open water, and a suburban landscape that has its own honest charm. A side of the capital most visitors never think to look for.

practical.how_to_get_there: By car or bus from central Minsk (~10km north).
practical.what_to_bring: SUP board or rent on site, sunscreen.
